questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A heavy stone is projected from a cliff of height $h$ in a specified direction. The speed at which it strikes the ground
options: must depend on the speed of projection
may be smaller than the speed of projection
must be larger than the speed of projection
must be independent of the speed of projection
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the heavy stone be $m$, the speed of projection be $u$, and the height of the cliff be $h$.

By the principle of conservation of mechanical energy, the total energy of the stone at the point of projection is equal to the total energy just before it strikes the ground.

Initial mechanical energy, $E_i = \dfrac{1}{2}mu^2 + mgh$

Final mechanical energy, $E_f = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$

Equating the initial and final energies:

$\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mu^2 + mgh$

$v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$

$v = \sqrt{u^2 + 2gh}$

From the above expression, it is clear that the final speed $v$ depends on the initial speed of projection $u$.

Since $h > 0$ and $g > 0$, we have $v^2 > u^2$, which implies $v > u$.

Thus, the speed at which the stone strikes the ground must be larger than the speed of projection.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ is tied to a light string of length $l$ having its other end fixed. The string is initially held horizontal, and the particle is projected with an upward velocity $v$. The particle is just able to complete a circle.
options: The initial kinetic energy of the particle was $\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = mgl$
The string becomes slack as the particle reaches its highest point
The particle's velocity drops to zero at the highest point
The particle passes through its initial position once again
questions.solution:
Let the fixed end of the string be the origin. The initial position of the particle is at a horizontal distance $l$ from the fixed end. The particle is projected upwards, so it travels a vertical distance $l$ to reach the highest point of the circular path.

For the particle to just complete the vertical circle, the tension in the string at the highest point must be exactly zero. This means the string momentarily becomes slack at the highest point.

At the highest point, the centripetal force is provided entirely by the weight of the particle:

$\dfrac{mv_H^2}{l} = mg \Rightarrow v_H = \sqrt{gl}$

Thus, the velocity at the highest point is $\sqrt{gl}$ and not zero.

Applying conservation of mechanical energy between the initial horizontal position and the highest point:

$\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_H^2 + mgl$

$\dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}m(gl) + mgl = \dfrac{3}{2}mgl$

The initial kinetic energy is $\dfrac{3}{2}mgl$, so the given statement for initial kinetic energy is incorrect.

Since the particle just completes the circle, it continues its circular motion and will pass through its initial position once again.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Zero work is performed by a force on an object if
options: the force remains perpendicular to its acceleration at all times
the force remains perpendicular to its velocity at all times
the object moves such that the point of application of the force remains stationary
the object stays stationary while the point of application of the force moves along the object
questions.solution:
The work done by a force on an object is defined as $W = \int \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{r}$, where $d\vec{r}$ is the displacement of the material point of the object at which the force acts.

If the force is perpendicular to the acceleration, it is not necessarily perpendicular to the velocity. For instance, a force acting on a particle moving with a velocity that has components both parallel and perpendicular to the force can still do work, even if the net acceleration is purely perpendicular to the force. Hence, work is not guaranteed to be zero in this case.

If the force remains perpendicular to the velocity at all times, the instantaneous power delivered is $P = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$. Since work is the integral of power over time, the work performed is exactly zero.

If the object moves such that the point of application of the force remains stationary, the displacement of the specific material point where the force acts is zero ($d\vec{r} = 0$). A common example is the normal force exerted by a fixed hinge on a swinging door. Since there is no displacement of the point of application, the work done is zero.

If the object remains strictly stationary, all of its material points are at rest. Even if the spatial point of application of the force moves along the object (for example, kinetic friction acting on a fixed floor when a block slides over it), the displacement of any material point of the object is zero. Thus, $d\vec{r} = 0$ and the mechanical work done on the object is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The total work performed on a particle is equal to the change in its kinetic energy
options: only if gravitational force alone acts on it
in all cases
only if the forces acting on it are conservative
only if elastic force alone acts on it
questions.solution:
According to the work-energy theorem, the total work done by all the forces acting on a particle is equal to the change in its kinetic energy.

$W_{\text{net}} = \Delta K$

This theorem is valid for all types of forces, including conservative, non-conservative, internal, and external forces. Therefore, it holds true in all cases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person lifts a suitcase from the floor and places it on a table. The work done by the person on the suitcase does not depend on
options: the time taken by the person to do so
the weight of the person
the weight of the suitcase
the path taken by the suitcase
questions.solution:
By the work-energy theorem, the net work done on the suitcase is equal to its change in kinetic energy.

Since the suitcase starts from rest on the floor and comes to rest on the table, the change in kinetic energy is zero.

$W_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{person}} + W_{\text{gravity}} = 0$

$W_{\text{person}} = -W_{\text{gravity}}$

The work done by gravity is $-mgh$, where $mg$ is the weight of the suitcase and $h$ is the height of the table.

$W_{\text{person}} = mgh$

This shows that the work done by the person on the suitcase depends only on the weight of the suitcase and the vertical displacement.

It does not depend on the time taken to lift it, the weight of the person, or the path taken by the suitcase (since gravity is a conservative force).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two observers are moving relative to one another at a speed $v$ along a straight line. They watch a block of mass $m$ travelling a distance $l$ over a rough surface. Which of the following quantities will be identical as measured by the two observers?
options: Work done by friction
Total work done on the block
Acceleration of the block
Kinetic energy of the block at time $t$
questions.solution:
Since the two observers are moving relative to each other at a constant speed $v$, they are in different inertial frames of reference.

According to Galilean relativity, the acceleration of an object is invariant across all inertial frames. If $u$ and $u'$ are the velocities of the block measured by the two observers, they are related by $u' = u - v$. Differentiating with respect to time $t$, we get $\dfrac{du'}{dt} = \dfrac{du}{dt}$ since $v$ is constant. Thus, $a' = a$.

The displacement of the block depends on the frame of reference. Consequently, the work done by friction ($W = \int F \cdot dx$) and the total work done will be different for the two observers.

Similarly, the kinetic energy ($K = \dfrac{1}{2}mu^2$) depends on the observer's frame since velocity is a relative quantity.

Therefore, only the acceleration of the block will be identical as measured by both observers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is subjected to a force of constant magnitude that is always perpendicular to its velocity. The motion of the particle occurs in a plane. It then follows that
options: it moves in a circular path
its velocity is constant
its acceleration is constant
its kinetic energy is constant
questions.solution:
Since the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, the power delivered by the force is zero ($\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$).

Therefore, the work done by the force is zero, which implies that the kinetic energy and the speed $v$ of the particle remain constant.

The magnitude of the force is constant, so the magnitude of the acceleration $a = \dfrac{F}{m}$ is also constant.

Since the force is perpendicular to the velocity, the acceleration is purely centripetal and is given by $a = \dfrac{v^2}{R}$, where $R$ is the radius of curvature.

Because both $v$ and $a$ are constant, the radius of curvature $R$ is constant. Motion in a plane with a constant radius of curvature is a circular path.

Since the direction of motion changes continuously, the velocity and acceleration vectors are not constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light spring with spring constant $k$ has one end attached to a wall and the other end connected to a block resting on a smooth horizontal surface. During a displacement, the work done by the spring is $\dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$. The possible situations are
options: the spring was initially stretched by a distance $x$ and finally returned to its natural length
the spring was initially at its natural length and finally reached a compressed position
the spring was initially at its natural length and finally reached a stretched position
the spring was initially compressed by a distance $x$ and finally returned to its natural length
questions.solution:
The work done by the spring force is given by $W = \dfrac{1}{2}kx_i^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}kx_f^2$, where $x_i$ and $x_f$ are the initial and final deformations (elongation or compression) of the spring from its natural length.

Given that the work done by the spring is $W = \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$, we have:

$\dfrac{1}{2}kx_i^2 - \dfrac{1}{2}kx_f^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$

For the spring initially stretched by a distance $x$ and finally returning to its natural length, $x_i = x$ and $x_f = 0$. Substituting these values, we get $W = \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2 - 0 = \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$.

For the spring initially compressed by a distance $x$ and finally returning to its natural length, $x_i = -x$ and $x_f = 0$. Substituting these values, we get $W = \dfrac{1}{2}k(-x)^2 - 0 = \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$.

For the spring initially at its natural length and finally reaching a compressed or stretched position by a distance $x$, $x_i = 0$ and $x_f = \pm x$. The work done is $W = 0 - \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2 = -\dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$.

Thus, the situations where the spring was initially stretched or compressed by a distance $x$ and finally returned to its natural length are the correct ones.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $M$ is suspended over a smooth and light pulley via a light string. A constant force $F$ pulls the other end of the string. Over a duration of $1 	ext{ s}$, the kinetic energy of the block increases by $20 	ext{ J}$.
options: During the $1 	ext{ s}$ interval, the work done by the force of gravity is $-20 	ext{ J}$.
During the $1 	ext{ s}$ interval, the work done on the block by the tension is $20 	ext{ J}$.
The tension in the string equals $F$.
The tension in the string equals $Mg$.
questions.solution:
Since the string is light and the pulley is smooth and light, the tension is uniform throughout the string. The force $F$ is applied at one end of the string, so the tension $T$ in the string is equal to the applied force $F$.

According to the work-energy theorem, the net work done on the block is equal to the change in its kinetic energy:

$W_{\text{net}} = W_T + W_g = \Delta K = 20 \text{ J}$

Since the block's kinetic energy increases, it has a net acceleration, meaning the net force is non-zero. Therefore, $T \neq Mg$.

The exact values of the work done by tension $W_T$ and the work done by gravity $W_g$ cannot be determined without additional information, but their sum is $20 \text{ J}$. Thus, the only definitively correct statement is that the tension in the string equals $F$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The kinetic energy of a particle increases continuously over time.
options: The magnitude of its linear momentum increases continuously.
The resultant force on the particle must always form an angle of less than $90^\circ$ with the velocity.
Its height above ground level must continuously decrease.
The resultant force on the particle must be parallel to its velocity at all instants.
questions.solution:
The kinetic energy of a particle is given by $K = \dfrac{p^2}{2m}$.

Since the kinetic energy increases continuously, the magnitude of its linear momentum $p$ must also increase continuously.

The rate of change of kinetic energy is equal to the power delivered by the net force, which is given by $\dfrac{dK}{dt} = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v} = F v \cos \theta$.

For the kinetic energy to increase continuously, $\dfrac{dK}{dt} > 0$, which implies $\cos \theta > 0$.

Thus, the angle $\theta$ between the resultant force and the velocity must always be less than $90^\circ$.

The height of the particle does not necessarily decrease, as an upward force greater than gravity can increase both its height and kinetic energy.

The resultant force need not be strictly parallel to the velocity; it only requires a positive tangential component.
